summary: Cathy O'Neil explains Andrew Gelman's visualization about statistical power and p-values using a hypothetical study of breast-fed versus bottle-fed babies. She demonstrates how a low-power test (0.06) produces wildly imprecise estimates that could easily give the wrong sign for a true effect. The post argues that when tests are underpowered, 'statistically significant' results actually mean the effect is being wildly overestimated, and even significant results have about a one-in-four chance of being completely wrong. She notes that researchers are expected to achieve 80% power but likely fudge their estimates, and that detecting small effects requires far more data than typically collected. The core problem: academic publishing requires statistical significance, incentivizing the use and misrepresentation of crappy tests.
key_points: A test with 0.06 power has about 45% of its distribution in wrong-sign territory, making it essentially useless for detecting small true effects
'Statistical significance' with a crappy test means you're wildly overestimating the actual effect size
Even statistically significant results from low-power tests have roughly a 1-in-4 chance of being completely wrong in direction
Researchers are incentivized to claim 80% power but likely fudge these estimates since publishing requires statistical significance
Detecting small effects requires far more data than most studies collect, but this reality conflicts with publication pressures
money_quotes: In fact, it looks like a good 45% of the area under the curve is in negative territory, so the chances are really very good that the test estimate, at this level of crappiness, would give you the wrong sign. That's a terrible test!
First, that 'statistically significant' in the presence of a small actual effect and a crappy test means that we are wildly overestimating the effect.
When we get a result that seems 'statistically significant,' in the presence of a crappy test, it still has a one in four chance of being totally wrong.
In other words, when we have crappy tests, we just shouldn't be talking about statistical significance at all.
But of course, nobody can publish their results without statistical significance, so there's that.
In general it requires way more data to produce evidence of a small effect.
Since researchers are expected to estimate their power to be at least 0.80, there's probably fudging going on and we might be trusting tests to be less crappy than they actually are.
key_insight: Statistical significance becomes meaningless and misleading when tests lack adequate power, yet publication incentives ensure researchers keep chasing it anyway.
